speaker: Charles S. Winner
rights: University of Baltimore, educational and research use
text: My name is Talmich Forte III. Today is December 16, 1998. I'm a student at the University of Baltimore History Department under grant. Today I will be conducting an interview with Mr. Charles Winner, Attorney at Law. Mr. Winner is currently representing former players of the Negro Baseball League. Baltimore City had two teams, the Baltimore Black Sox and the E-Lite Giants. Testing, one, two, three, testing. Today is December 16, 1998. My name is Talmich Forte. I will be interviewing Mr. Charles Winner, Attorney at Law. Mr. Winner, what is your full name, sir? Charles asked Winner. What is your address? You can address it at the farm, please. 315 North Charles Street, Baltimore 21201. And the phone number, sir? 410352000. Are you married, sir? Indeed. And your wife today, miss? Judy, J U D I. Okay. You have any children, sir? One son, Eric, ERIC, who's 30 years old. Okay. Are your parents living, sir? Yes, my mother. Okay. Her name, sir? Betty, B-E-T-T-Y. Okay. Do you have any siblings? One brother, Jacob, J-A-C-O-B. Okay, sir. You don't have the answers if you want to, but they want to know what's your race, religious, and ethnic background? I have no trouble with that. I'm a Jewish Caucasian. Okay. Are you a native Baltimore, sir? Absolutely. And while we know what your occupation is, where were you educated, sir? I'm sorry, secondary college. I was educated at the University of Maryland, the University of Baltimore, and the University of Baltimore Law School. Okay. What is your relationship to sports in Baltimore, sir? I am General Counsel to the Negro League baseball players of America. Okay. And while you just answer the next question, what is your relationship to Negro baseball league? Okay. Are you representing any former Negro players at this time? Yes, but I would not disclose their names. Okay, sir. Okay. And... But... Okay. There's... There's... There's... There's... There's... Players do have certain benefits, or some of the... Groups are pushing to make a relief? Yes. Well, there's an issue as to whether or not they are entitled to punches or not, and certain of them are entitled to receive medical benefits and punch and benefits from major league baseball, depending on meeting the requirements of the major league baseball. And one of those requirements, sir. I'm not thoroughly familiar with that, but I believe that the main requirements for punches and benefits was that they had played for three consecutive years prior to 1950. Okay. Excuse me. And what obstacles have you encountered if any working with the pensions or the associations? Well, I have not represented their interests as such regarding their pensions or their health benefits, which preceded my representation. I was not the first counsel that they had. This organization has only been around recently, recently since 1990 or 1991. And prior to that time, I don't believe that they had any representation. This association was started by a channel one named Richard Burr, the B-E-R-G, I think. And Edward Schalter, and they were initially represented by a very large New York law firm. They had great difficulties with their prior executive directors or presidents, as the case may be, whether it be Richard Burr or Juan James Jackson, which caused them some difficulties. And I've been instrumental in trying to overcome those difficulties and hopefully put them on a firm or financial footing. Okay. Are your legal efforts confined to the former New Replayers in Baltimore or is it nationally? Anyone that belongs to the organization or wherever they may be. And to my knowledge, very few of them are involved in more. Mr. Burr is in Baltimore and there may be two or three others, but that's the extent that I know. Are there any litigation and process that you could discuss? No. Okay. Have there been any types of settlements that you can talk about them? No. Okay. Why was the Negro basically different from other leagues, especially other black leagues? Well, I don't know that there were other black leagues as such. The history of the Negro League Baseball is somewhat scattered. To my knowledge, most of it has been east of the Mississippi River. Most prominently, there's to talk about what took place in the 20s and 30s and 40s. There's not too much talk prior to 1920 or after 1950. Much of it were pickup games. Much of it was dictated by the expanse. They were not the types of teams that had the money to fly, because they're to train or even move, because they're to cause. I don't know any of the Negro League players that got rich playing ball. Okay. What was the independent traveling team, if you familiar with that? I heard someone named route that once. Well, I don't know about an independent traveling team, but they were frequently all star teams that barns stormed and tried to get a game where they could. They would play, for example, in Philadelphia, and their manager would be on the phone while they were playing trying to get a game the next day with some team. But the Negro League's themselves were, in fact, a defined league. They had specific teams. They had all star games which were played. And all of that came to an end in 1950. Many people confuse Negro League's with black ball teams. There is a distinction. The Negro League's were a defined league with its rules and regulations and so forth to my knowledge. Whereas after they ended, there were still black ball teams playing one another, but that was not the Negro League. Okay. Well, how many teams did both go ahead? Baltimore had two well-known teams, the most recent being the Baltimore League giants. And prior to that, there was the Baltimore Black socks. Who was the owners of the Black Sacks and the League Giants, if you know? Okay. Can you tell me about this post on the wall here? I have a poster from a reunion of the Negro League baseball players in the fall of 1990. They had been reunions since then. They tried to get together once a year or so. And generally, centered around some sports memorabilia or baseball cards show most recently they've had such a meeting in Chantilly, Virginia. They get together as many of the people as are healthy and well enough to come. And that is becoming increasingly more difficult. They also have the Major League's has an Assistance Program where they will assist some of these players that may be having difficulties of one kind or another. But in 1990, as you can see, some former Major Liggers were there, Monti Irvin and Joe Black. And they had some Leon Day who was deceased. Yes. Who is deceased but in the Hall of Fame. Double duty Radcliffe, Buck Leonard, Max Manning, Cool Pop of Bell, Lester Lockert, just a Gene Bonson, Stan Lake Lahn, Wilmer Fields, who Wilmer is the current president of the Negro League baseball players association. And just a very genuine good group of men. And meeting these men that you ever discussed, have you ever witnessed a Negro-based football league, gained yourself or? No. I'm old but I'm not that old. Okay. Now, you mentioned some of the guys that you met and what was your impression of some of these gentlemen? Well, they were, I think that you have said it when you used the word gentlemen. To a man, they are gentlemen. They are well-spoken people. They are honest. They have many fond and good memories of baseball. They played because they loved the game and because they enjoyed the game, because they enjoyed playing with one another. And they endured any number of hardships in order to do it. They did, when they were playing, they did not stay in the best hotels. They were not able to eat in the best restaurants. They generally had a bus that they traveled on and they would sleep on the bus. And they are not the kind of, they played in ball parks, which did not have manicured grounds. But they were all genuinely most of them of great talent and ability. Okay. Understand that Blacks are actually a lot of Johns played, you know, exhibition games or all-star games against like the Orioles or other major league white teams. You know anything about that? Not a great deal. They did play or they did play against all-star teams. Going back years ago, Babe Ruth may have had an all-star team. And there may have been other players that in the off season had all-star teams that played against Negro League All-Stars. And from what I understand, the Negro League players fared rather well. Okay. You know anything about Worry Cup and Nella? Worry Cup and Nella prior to going to the major leagues. I believe probably in the late 1930s played for the Baltimore League Chains. And it was just an incredible player and a wonderful man. Okay. Now, Mr. Sam Lacey is currently a still ace. What's right for the Afro-American? Have you ever met Mr. Lacey? I've met Mr. Lacey at that that he would remember me. And I think that he's been just a wonderful for the sport of baseball and wonderful for the black ball players. Okay. Let's see Lianne Day. You just mentioned that he's deceased. I understand that they wrecked constructing a park and is honored. Are you familiar with this? Yes, I am. A gentleman by the name of Robert or Dr. Bob Heranomus. Been very instrumental in doing that. And has also been very instrumental in helping the Negro League ball players and perpetuating their memory. Okay. Do you know where the park would be located? Again, memory doesn't serve. I could get there if you wanted me to take you. But I can't exactly tell you where it is. Well, yeah, I heard rumors somewhere around Lincoln Park. It's in that general area. Now, baseball players of the day and baseball players of the Negro League, do you think of these guys who are living today? Do you think the current ball players, or even ball players of that era could fare well against them? I think that many of them is playing today and playing today when they were in their prime, would be superstars. They would be every bit the equivalent of some of the great white ball players and some of the great black ball players of the Willie Mays and Henry Arons and the Ernie Banks of the world. They would compete more than favorably. Okay. Excuse me. Now, since the 50s and a lot of blacks say migrated into the white baseball leagues, do you think that that hurt some of the other players that wasn't picked up, or were too old to pick up? I don't think that that was hurting them. Unfortunately, I think that was just a fact of life that the opportunities became available when they were too old physically to be able to do it. Whether or not they could have served in some other capacity, I don't know. I tend to think that they could, whether it was coaches or something of the sort. Many of them did, I think, so. Others, it was beyond the pale for them. Time had gone by. But certainly. I don't think that they were hurt to the extent that they were denied an opportunity. But that could be true of many. Okay. Do you think that, even though it was denied the opportunity, that a lot of them, even though they worked outside of baseball, that a lot of them spent more time trying to raise families, because of the low wages they were paying, playing baseball? Sure. I mean, they had an obligation to their families, and they loved the sport. They continued to love the sport. Each and every one of them to this day. Most of the ones, they are very active in their local communities with the children. They go to the schools. They talk about baseball. They let them see them. They tell them of their experiences. And they are extremely valuable and helpful to the youth. Yes. Well, I read somewhere that Joe Black was a graduate of Howard University. And he also had Dennis Tier and Marilyn Baltimore. Are you familiar with that? I mean, I've met Joe Black a number of times, and I know that Joe is also active with major league baseball players. He was a major league baseball player, and a rather good one at that. Okay. Are there any other former players here in Baltimore that you're aware of that's, you know, really active in the sport? Well, I'll... What's the sport today? You have Mr. Burke, who played for the Baltimore League Giants, and who today is a tennis instructor, and has a very nice following. And there may be a few others that just don't come to mind, if they're not. Okay. Remember also that most of these players, the youngest of them now, are 74 or 75 years old, and because of their health, they are each year there's fewer of them, and the time is not too far off when there won't be any of them. Okay. The New Cannon Station Baseball Museum, they're hopefully to put up a, they will, they will leave exhibition. Do you think there's enough material left around to present this to the public? There's no question in my mind that there is. Whether the people that have it want to give it, loan it, donate it, make it available, maybe another question. But I think that there is plenty that is available, yes. Mr. Wiener, I'd ask you if there, if you thought there was enough material for an exhibit, for any more baseball league, any response was that you thought it, that was the people who wants to donate it. Sure. In other words, I'm not so sure how much material is still owned by the players themselves. First of all, much of this stuff was given away when they thought it was of little or no value. Value has only become something of recent interest. But they've given away their uniforms, their gloves, their balls, their bats, and so forth. Someone has it. Whether it's family members, some of it of course may be in Cooperstown, some of it may be in the Kansas City Museum, or one or more other types of places of that sort. But there is still equipment around in people that have it that, as I said, would in one way or another make it available to the Baltimore Museum. Additionally, there are photographs. There is correspondence. There are all sorts of schedules and printed paper types of things, newspapers and of that nature. There are maybe stock certificates from some of these teams. There may be motion pictures. Particularly, there is a film which is known to exist known as the Quincy Troop film, which is an interview in, I don't know when it was, 1990 or 1991, of many of the players as to their experiences and what they knew. There's things of this nature that are around. Do you think a exhibit of this nature would help baseball in Baltimore or hinder it? I think it would most definitely help baseball in Baltimore. I think it would enormously help the Bay of Ruth Museum, and I think it would have great attendance and great interest. Okay. As a Baltimoreian myself, I was a little not even about the neighborhood baseball league. Do you think a lot of Baltimoreians are as just as not even as I? Yes. And I think many major leagueers are just as naive as you, and many baseball people are just as naive of you. The point that I made was that the Negro leagues and black ball players are not the same thing. And it is that about which there has been some confusion. There is one and only one official Negro league. There is one and only one official Negro league baseball players association of Negro league players, and this is it. There may be pretenders, but this is it. This is the real thing. And then the original, do you have a date from when it began and when it ended? When it began, I don't recall, but as I said, it came to an end in 1950. After 1951, there was no more Negro league baseball. Do you think that presenting black Negro league exhibition here in Baltimore, do you think that they may have a traveling exhibit, maybe one to schools? There are a lot of people who don't know and will make kids. As I said, I think that lots of the ball players that are able to do so do go to the schools now. And I think it is very helpful, particularly to the predominantly schools that have a predominantly black enrollment. I think that students relate to these players. Additionally, I think that it would be appropriate for the schools perhaps to have bus trips. And I think there will probably be children that are bus to the ballpark and to the museum to see and hear and ask questions. Sure. It's one's heritage. Now, the Camden Yards station, not only the house, the Negro league exhibits, but they also have other exhibits there. Do you think this is a good move for baseball in Baltimore, per se? Absolutely. I think one will enhance the other. So you feel someone going to the game, goes to the museum, watch the game, they will have a more fall enlightenment of baseball. Sure. And they can have discussions. I mean, there are discussions now about what was the greatest baseball team of all time. Who was the greatest player of all time? Would Babe Ruth still been the giant that he was if he was playing today? They had different rules, somewhat then, different balls, different bats, and all those discussions are very interesting. And we all tend to know what we know right now without knowing much about history. And as more time goes by and you have less people that actually saw someone play, I mean, just today, there are many people in the American league cities that have no appreciation for the players in the national league. Because they don't see them. They read about them. Maybe they'll, you have television, but you don't see that many national league games here in Baltimore, certainly. Every now and then maybe. Well, do you think television would have made a difference back in the 30s, 40s and 50s, as far as the Negro league is concerned, or even announcements on radio? Sure. Anything would have made a difference. It's fun and interesting. Well, for me, I find interesting that more, I talk about it, talking to you and more read and more learn. And about players and these guys, how they play, and I don't think it's, well, the next question is, do you know if there's any real statistical data on it, or record keeping up them? Sure. There have been books that have been written. I don't know how accurate the books are. Many of the players will tell you that the books are not that accurate because they were never consulted about it. Many of the players will tell you that they feel that the Ken Burns documentary on baseball, as it pertained to the Negro leagues, could have been much better than it was, and that many of the players who could have helped things were not really part of that. They will also tell you that anyone that saw the movie, then go long in the traveling all stars, doesn't know anything because that's not what Negro league baseball was. That was what Hollywood thinks it is. Okay. Now, I see that you have baseball profanity around you, off as a big baseball fan. I'm a fan, and I'm a collector. Okay. How long is your collection? I have a very nice collection, and I have things that may not be of great money value, but are of great interest in sentimental value. I think that's perhaps one of the true definitions of a collector, not someone who was collecting stuff because they think it's worth a whole lot of money, but because it's of interest to them. And you take a, I mean, I have, for example, a friend that has a picture on his wall when he was a little boy with a Babe Ruth's arm around him. I don't know how much that's worth, but it's worth a million dollars to that man. The average collector is not, cannot afford the lineup card from Paul Ripkins record game. The average fan cannot afford Babe Ruth's uniform. I mean, these things may go for hundreds of thousands of dollars, but there are many collectors that have many things that they do collect. They collect because it's their favorite player, and they want to try to have many things that they can have in connection with that player. So there's all different ways of collecting. Okay. Do you have anything from the Negro Leads? I do. I have some very interesting things. I have some correspondence, which is very interesting. I'm going back into these 40s about pick up games. Of course, I've got photographs and other items, yes. What do you loan these to the museum or sell them or donate them? Well, I don't know at this stage in my life that I want to sell much of anything, but if I had of anything that was of interest to the museum, I would have been able to have some of that kind of quality and they asked me about it. I'd make it available to them. Well, I want to thank you for this interview. It's very interesting. And it's really been nice talking to you. I appreciate having been asked, and I hope that I've had a little bit to whatever the ultimate product of these interviews will be. I hope to hear your voice in the Kansas Museum, sir. Thank you very much. Thank you sir.

segments.text: My name is Talmich Forte III.
Today is December 16, 1998.
I'm a student at the University of Baltimore History Department under grant.
Today I will be conducting an interview with Mr. Charles Winner, Attorney at Law.
Mr. Winner is currently representing former players of the Negro Baseball League.
Baltimore City had two teams, the Baltimore Black Sox and the E-Lite Giants.
Testing, one, two, three, testing.
Today is December 16, 1998.
My name is Talmich Forte.
I will be interviewing Mr. Charles Winner, Attorney at Law.
Mr. Winner, what is your full name, sir?
Charles asked Winner.
What is your address?
You can address it at the farm, please.
315 North Charles Street, Baltimore 21201.
And the phone number, sir?
Are you married, sir?
Indeed.
And your wife today, miss?
Judy, J U D I.
Okay.
You have any children, sir?
One son, Eric, ERIC, who's 30 years old.
Okay.
Are your parents living, sir?
Yes, my mother.
Okay.
Her name, sir?
Betty, B-E-T-T-Y.
Okay.
Do you have any siblings?
One brother, Jacob, J-A-C-O-B.
Okay, sir.
You don't have the answers if you want to, but they want to know what's your race, religious,
and ethnic background?
I have no trouble with that.
I'm a Jewish Caucasian.
Okay.
Are you a native Baltimore, sir?
Absolutely.
And while we know what your occupation is, where were you educated, sir?
I'm sorry, secondary college.
I was educated at the University of Maryland, the University of Baltimore, and the University
of Baltimore Law School.
Okay.
What is your relationship to sports in Baltimore, sir?
I am General Counsel to the Negro League baseball players of America.
Okay.
And while you just answer the next question, what is your relationship to Negro baseball league?
Okay.
Are you representing any former Negro players at this time?
Yes, but I would not disclose their names.
Okay, sir.
Okay.
And...
But...
Okay.
There's...
There's...
There's...
There's...
There's...
Players do have certain benefits, or some of the...
Groups are pushing to make a relief?
Yes.
Well, there's an issue as to whether or not they are entitled to punches or not, and certain
of them are entitled to receive medical benefits and punch and benefits from major league baseball,
depending on meeting the requirements of the major league baseball.
And one of those requirements, sir.
I'm not thoroughly familiar with that, but I believe that the main requirements for
punches and benefits was that they had played for three consecutive years prior to 1950.
Okay.
Excuse me.
And what obstacles have you encountered if any working with the pensions or the associations?
Well, I have not represented their interests as such regarding their pensions or their health
benefits, which preceded my representation.
I was not the first counsel that they had.
This organization has only been around recently, recently since 1990 or 1991.
And prior to that time, I don't believe that they had any representation.
This association was started by a channel one named Richard Burr, the B-E-R-G, I think.
And Edward Schalter, and they were initially represented by a very large New York law firm.
They had great difficulties with their prior executive directors or presidents, as the case may be,
whether it be Richard Burr or Juan James Jackson, which caused them some difficulties.
And I've been instrumental in trying to overcome those difficulties and hopefully put them on a firm or financial footing.
Okay.
Are your legal efforts confined to the former New Replayers in Baltimore or is it nationally?
Anyone that belongs to the organization or wherever they may be.
And to my knowledge, very few of them are involved in more.
Mr. Burr is in Baltimore and there may be two or three others, but that's the extent that I know.
Are there any litigation and process that you could discuss?
No.
Okay.
Have there been any types of settlements that you can talk about them?
No.
Okay.
Why was the Negro basically different from other leagues, especially other black leagues?
Well, I don't know that there were other black leagues as such.
The history of the Negro League Baseball is somewhat scattered.
To my knowledge, most of it has been east of the Mississippi River.
Most prominently, there's to talk about what took place in the 20s and 30s and 40s.
There's not too much talk prior to 1920 or after 1950.
Much of it were pickup games.
Much of it was dictated by the expanse.
They were not the types of teams that had the money to fly, because they're to train or even move, because they're to cause.
I don't know any of the Negro League players that got rich playing ball.
Okay.
What was the independent traveling team, if you familiar with that?
I heard someone named route that once.
Well, I don't know about an independent traveling team, but they were frequently all star teams that barns stormed and tried to get a game where they could.
They would play, for example, in Philadelphia, and their manager would be on the phone while they were playing trying to get a game the next day with some team.
But the Negro League's themselves were, in fact, a defined league.
They had specific teams. They had all star games which were played.
And all of that came to an end in 1950.
Many people confuse Negro League's with black ball teams.
There is a distinction. The Negro League's were a defined league with its rules and regulations and so forth to my knowledge.
Whereas after they ended, there were still black ball teams playing one another, but that was not the Negro League.
Okay.
Well, how many teams did both go ahead?
Baltimore had two well-known teams, the most recent being the Baltimore League giants.
And prior to that, there was the Baltimore Black socks.
Who was the owners of the Black Sacks and the League Giants, if you know?
Okay. Can you tell me about this post on the wall here?
I have a poster from a reunion of the Negro League baseball players in the fall of 1990.
They had been reunions since then. They tried to get together once a year or so.
And generally, centered around some sports memorabilia or baseball cards show most recently they've had such a meeting in Chantilly, Virginia.
They get together as many of the people as are healthy and well enough to come.
And that is becoming increasingly more difficult.
They also have the Major League's has an Assistance Program where they will assist some of these players that may be having difficulties of one kind or another.
But in 1990, as you can see, some former Major Liggers were there, Monti Irvin and Joe Black.
And they had some Leon Day who was deceased.
Yes. Who is deceased but in the Hall of Fame.
Double duty Radcliffe, Buck Leonard, Max Manning, Cool Pop of Bell, Lester Lockert,
just a Gene Bonson, Stan Lake Lahn, Wilmer Fields, who Wilmer is the current president of the Negro League baseball players association.
And just a very genuine good group of men.
And meeting these men that you ever discussed, have you ever witnessed a Negro-based football league, gained yourself or?
No.
I'm old but I'm not that old.
Okay.
Now, you mentioned some of the guys that you met and what was your impression of some of these gentlemen?
Well, they were, I think that you have said it when you used the word gentlemen.
To a man, they are gentlemen.
They are well-spoken people.
They are honest. They have many fond and good memories of baseball.
They played because they loved the game and because they enjoyed the game, because they enjoyed playing with one another.
And they endured any number of hardships in order to do it.
They did, when they were playing, they did not stay in the best hotels.
They were not able to eat in the best restaurants.
They generally had a bus that they traveled on and they would sleep on the bus.
And they are not the kind of, they played in ball parks, which did not have manicured grounds.
But they were all genuinely most of them of great talent and ability.
Okay. Understand that Blacks are actually a lot of Johns played, you know, exhibition games or all-star games against like the Orioles or other major league white teams.
You know anything about that?
Not a great deal. They did play or they did play against all-star teams.
Going back years ago, Babe Ruth may have had an all-star team.
And there may have been other players that in the off season had all-star teams that played against Negro League All-Stars.
And from what I understand, the Negro League players fared rather well.
Okay. You know anything about Worry Cup and Nella?
Worry Cup and Nella prior to going to the major leagues.
I believe probably in the late 1930s played for the Baltimore League Chains.
And it was just an incredible player and a wonderful man.
Okay. Now, Mr. Sam Lacey is currently a still ace.
What's right for the Afro-American? Have you ever met Mr. Lacey?
I've met Mr. Lacey at that that he would remember me.
And I think that he's been just a wonderful for the sport of baseball and wonderful for the black ball players.
Okay. Let's see Lianne Day. You just mentioned that he's deceased.
I understand that they wrecked constructing a park and is honored. Are you familiar with this?
Yes, I am. A gentleman by the name of Robert or Dr. Bob Heranomus.
Been very instrumental in doing that.
And has also been very instrumental in helping the Negro League ball players and perpetuating their memory.
Okay. Do you know where the park would be located?
Again, memory doesn't serve. I could get there if you wanted me to take you.
But I can't exactly tell you where it is.
Well, yeah, I heard rumors somewhere around Lincoln Park.
It's in that general area.
Now, baseball players of the day and baseball players of the Negro League,
do you think of these guys who are living today? Do you think the current ball players,
or even ball players of that era could fare well against them?
I think that many of them is playing today and playing today when they were in their prime,
would be superstars.
They would be every bit the equivalent of some of the great white ball players
and some of the great black ball players of the Willie Mays and Henry Arons and the Ernie Banks of the world.
They would compete more than favorably.
Okay. Excuse me.
Now, since the 50s and a lot of blacks say migrated into the white baseball leagues,
do you think that that hurt some of the other players that wasn't picked up,
or were too old to pick up?
I don't think that that was hurting them.
Unfortunately, I think that was just a fact of life that the opportunities became available
when they were too old physically to be able to do it.
Whether or not they could have served in some other capacity, I don't know.
I tend to think that they could, whether it was coaches or something of the sort.
Many of them did, I think, so.
Others, it was beyond the pale for them.
Time had gone by.
But certainly.
I don't think that they were hurt to the extent that they were denied an opportunity.
But that could be true of many.
Okay. Do you think that, even though it was denied the opportunity,
that a lot of them, even though they worked outside of baseball,
that a lot of them spent more time trying to raise families,
because of the low wages they were paying, playing baseball?
Sure.
I mean, they had an obligation to their families, and they loved the sport.
They continued to love the sport.
Each and every one of them to this day.
Most of the ones, they are very active in their local communities with the children.
They go to the schools.
They talk about baseball.
They let them see them.
They tell them of their experiences.
And they are extremely valuable and helpful to the youth.
Yes.
Well, I read somewhere that Joe Black was a graduate of Howard University.
And he also had Dennis Tier and Marilyn Baltimore.
Are you familiar with that?
I mean, I've met Joe Black a number of times, and I know that Joe is also active
with major league baseball players.
He was a major league baseball player, and a rather good one at that.
Okay.
Are there any other former players here in Baltimore that you're aware of that's,
you know, really active in the sport?
Well, I'll...
What's the sport today?
You have Mr. Burke, who played for the Baltimore League Giants,
and who today is a tennis instructor,
and has a very nice following.
And there may be a few others that just don't come to mind, if they're not.
Okay.
Remember also that most of these players, the youngest of them now,
are 74 or 75 years old, and because of their health,
they are each year there's fewer of them,
and the time is not too far off when there won't be any of them.
Okay.
The New Cannon Station Baseball Museum, they're hopefully to put up a,
they will, they will leave exhibition.
Do you think there's enough material left around to present this to the public?
There's no question in my mind that there is.
Whether the people that have it want to give it,
loan it, donate it, make it available, maybe another question.
But I think that there is plenty that is available, yes.
Mr. Wiener, I'd ask you if there, if you thought there was enough material for an exhibit,
for any more baseball league, any response was that you thought it,
that was the people who wants to donate it.
Sure. In other words, I'm not so sure how much material is still owned by the players themselves.
First of all, much of this stuff was given away when they thought it was of little or no value.
Value has only become something of recent interest.
But they've given away their uniforms, their gloves, their balls, their bats, and so forth.
Someone has it. Whether it's family members, some of it of course may be in Cooperstown,
some of it may be in the Kansas City Museum, or one or more other types of places of that sort.
But there is still equipment around in people that have it that, as I said,
would in one way or another make it available to the Baltimore Museum.
Additionally, there are photographs. There is correspondence.
There are all sorts of schedules and printed paper types of things, newspapers and of that nature.
There are maybe stock certificates from some of these teams.
There may be motion pictures. Particularly, there is a film which is known to exist known as the Quincy Troop film,
which is an interview in, I don't know when it was, 1990 or 1991, of many of the players as to their experiences and what they knew.
There's things of this nature that are around.
Do you think a exhibit of this nature would help baseball in Baltimore or hinder it?
I think it would most definitely help baseball in Baltimore.
I think it would enormously help the Bay of Ruth Museum, and I think it would have great attendance and great interest.
Okay. As a Baltimoreian myself, I was a little not even about the neighborhood baseball league.
Do you think a lot of Baltimoreians are as just as not even as I?
Yes. And I think many major leagueers are just as naive as you, and many baseball people are just as naive of you.
The point that I made was that the Negro leagues and black ball players are not the same thing.
And it is that about which there has been some confusion.
There is one and only one official Negro league.
There is one and only one official Negro league baseball players association of Negro league players, and this is it.
There may be pretenders, but this is it. This is the real thing.
And then the original, do you have a date from when it began and when it ended?
When it began, I don't recall, but as I said, it came to an end in 1950.
After 1951, there was no more Negro league baseball.
Do you think that presenting black Negro league exhibition here in Baltimore, do you think that they may have a traveling exhibit, maybe one to schools?
There are a lot of people who don't know and will make kids.
As I said, I think that lots of the ball players that are able to do so do go to the schools now.
And I think it is very helpful, particularly to the predominantly schools that have a predominantly black enrollment.
I think that students relate to these players.
Additionally, I think that it would be appropriate for the schools perhaps to have bus trips.
And I think there will probably be children that are bus to the ballpark and to the museum to see and hear and ask questions.
Sure. It's one's heritage.
Now, the Camden Yards station, not only the house, the Negro league exhibits, but they also have other exhibits there.
Do you think this is a good move for baseball in Baltimore, per se?
Absolutely. I think one will enhance the other.
So you feel someone going to the game, goes to the museum, watch the game, they will have a more fall enlightenment of baseball.
Sure. And they can have discussions. I mean, there are discussions now about what was the greatest baseball team of all time.
Who was the greatest player of all time? Would Babe Ruth still been the giant that he was if he was playing today?
They had different rules, somewhat then, different balls, different bats, and all those discussions are very interesting.
And we all tend to know what we know right now without knowing much about history.

And as more time goes by and you have less people that actually saw someone play, I mean, just today, there are many people in the American league cities that have no appreciation for the players in the national league.
Because they don't see them. They read about them. Maybe they'll, you have television, but you don't see that many national league games here in Baltimore, certainly.
Every now and then maybe.
Well, do you think television would have made a difference back in the 30s, 40s and 50s, as far as the Negro league is concerned, or even announcements on radio?
Sure. Anything would have made a difference.
It's fun and interesting. Well, for me, I find interesting that more, I talk about it, talking to you and more read and more learn.
And about players and these guys, how they play, and I don't think it's, well, the next question is, do you know if there's any real statistical data on it, or record keeping up them?
Sure. There have been books that have been written. I don't know how accurate the books are.
Many of the players will tell you that the books are not that accurate because they were never consulted about it.

Many of the players will tell you that they feel that the Ken Burns documentary on baseball, as it pertained to the Negro leagues, could have been much better than it was, and that many of the players who could have helped things were not really part of that.

They will also tell you that anyone that saw the movie, then go long in the traveling all stars, doesn't know anything because that's not what Negro league baseball was. That was what Hollywood thinks it is.
Okay. Now, I see that you have baseball profanity around you, off as a big baseball fan.
I'm a fan, and I'm a collector.
Okay. How long is your collection?
I have a very nice collection, and I have things that may not be of great money value, but are of great interest in sentimental value.
I think that's perhaps one of the true definitions of a collector, not someone who was collecting stuff because they think it's worth a whole lot of money, but because it's of interest to them.
And you take a, I mean, I have, for example, a friend that has a picture on his wall when he was a little boy with a Babe Ruth's arm around him.
I don't know how much that's worth, but it's worth a million dollars to that man.
The average collector is not, cannot afford the lineup card from Paul Ripkins record game.
The average fan cannot afford Babe Ruth's uniform.
I mean, these things may go for hundreds of thousands of dollars, but there are many collectors that have many things that they do collect.
They collect because it's their favorite player, and they want to try to have many things that they can have in connection with that player.
So there's all different ways of collecting.
Okay. Do you have anything from the Negro Leads?
I do. I have some very interesting things. I have some correspondence, which is very interesting.
I'm going back into these 40s about pick up games. Of course, I've got photographs and other items, yes.
What do you loan these to the museum or sell them or donate them?
Well, I don't know at this stage in my life that I want to sell much of anything, but if I had of anything that was of interest to the museum,
I would have been able to have some of that kind of quality and they asked me about it. I'd make it available to them.
Well, I want to thank you for this interview.
It's very interesting. And it's really been nice talking to you.
I appreciate having been asked, and I hope that I've had a little bit to whatever the ultimate product of these interviews will be.
I hope to hear your voice in the Kansas Museum, sir.
Thank you very much. Thank you sir.
